tickets.title: Primitive byte arrays with a MIME type: a bytes Target kind (0.4, additive)
tickets.body:
## Proposal

Add an **additive** `Target` kind for raw binary payloads with an attached MIME type:

```ts
{ kind: "bytes", mime: string, value: Uint8Array }
```

- **Identity is the hash of the raw bytes.** Encoding is transport, never identity: base64url in JSON wire contexts, a native byte string in CBOR. This removes base64's canonicalization hazards (variant alphabets, padding) from the content address entirely.
- **MIME rides in-kind**, RDF-datatype style — attested interpretation-testimony that travels with the bytes, not a detachable sibling pointer. Two claims asserting the same bytes under different MIME types are different claims, which is honest.

## Motivation

Loam is converging on a doctrine of *code and documents shipped as deltas*: a delta asserts the content-addressed bytes of a coherent, versioned unit, and the signature attests exactly the bytes that run (Loam TODO, "The snapshot doctrine"). Text (ESM source) works today as a string primitive. But the renderer work coming in Loam §23 — UI components pushed as deltas, mounted by a stock host — wants **basic binary assets right in the hypergraph**: icons, small images, fonts, wasm. Today those mean base64-in-a-string: ~4/3 bloat, no typing, and an encoding sitting inside the content address.

A `bytes` primitive also gives every downstream surface what it needs: a REST door can answer with a correct `Content-Type`, an inspector can render "a 43 KB `image/png`" instead of a base64 wall, and sandbox/quarantine machinery gets a signal about what kind of thing it is holding.

## Compatibility

Additive, so the story is clean:

- **No migration.** Existing deltas do not change; old stores simply never contain the new kind. The new shape is unambiguously distinct from all prior versions by construction (a new `kind`), so shape-detection can never misfire.
- **Federation forward-compat**: a pre-0.4 peer pulling from a store holding `bytes` deltas refuses the unknown kind. That is version discipline, not breakage — and admission shape-requirements can express it explicitly.

## Non-goals / cautions

- **This is not a blob store.** A 10 MB image in a delta stresses every path — hyperview copies, federation offers, a browser peer's ~5 MB origin quota. The primitive is the *inline* rung of a storage ladder (inline bytes → content-addressed ref → chunked tree); large assets still climb the ladder, and doors may cap sizes as deployment config. The primitive's job is to make the inline rung honest and the reference rungs typed.
- The delta grammar stays small; this adds one kind, not a media subsystem.

## Open questions for the design

1. Exact canonical form: where the raw bytes enter the hash preimage, and the pinned JSON-wire encoding (base64url, unpadded?).
2. Conformance vectors: hash-stability vectors for the new kind across the TS and Rust implementations.
3. Any size guidance the substrate itself should state (likely none — a caps-are-deployment-config note may be enough).
4. Whether `mime` is required or defaults to `application/octet-stream`.

Proposed as rhizomatic 0.4, timed alongside Loam's §23 (renderers) design stage; nothing in Loam §21/§22 blocks on it.

🤖 Drafted with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) at Myk's request.

tickets.title: Add a Schema Schema (`SCHEMA_SCHEMA`) — self-hosting parity for the resolution Schema
tickets.body:
rhizomatic ships `HYPER_SCHEMA_SCHEMA` — the self-hosting definition of a **HyperSchema** — plus `publishSchemaClaims`/`loadSchema` to publish and load one as deltas. There is **no equivalent for the resolution `Schema`** (`{ props: Map<field, Policy>, default: Policy }`): no `SCHEMA_SCHEMA`, no at-rest delta vocabulary for a Schema, and the `Schema` type carries no name. A Schema today exists only inline (canonical JSON quoted inside a consumer's registration), never as a first-class, resolvable domain node.

**Why this is needed.** Loam's §21 (schema identity & versioning) makes a Schema a first-class entity — its own id, its own deltas, resolved from the ground exactly as a HyperSchema is — with registration demoted to a binding, so that many named lenses can live over one HyperSchema and each can be snapshotted into a pinnable `name@hash` version. That requires publishing/loading a Schema *as deltas, resolved by a Schema Schema*. The whole system rests on the symmetry `HyperSchema : HyperView :: Schema : View`; if one side of it has a self-hosting schema-schema and publish/load, the other side deserves the parallel for the identical reasons — self-description, portability, resolvability. Doing it in Loam instead would fork the substrate's self-hosting story at the application layer (a parallel `loam.schema.*`), exactly the near-synonym duplication the vocabulary discipline avoids.

**Proposed.** Add `SCHEMA_SCHEMA` (the self-hosting definition of a resolution Schema) and publish/load parity — either `publishSchemaClaims`/`loadSchema` equivalents for Schemas, or a generalization of the existing pair — with an at-rest delta vocabulary (`rhizomatic.schema.*`, parallel to `rhizomatic.hyperschema.*`).

**Coordination.** This blocks Loam §21, which already ships an on-wire migration (its `schema:`→`hyperschema:` entity rename). Ideally this lands in the **same release** as the naming reconciliation in #10, so Loam pulls one new rhizomatic and folds everything into §21's single migration wave rather than cutting a second one.

**A deeper question, noted not blocked (Myk).** Whether the query/resolution apparatus (schema-schemas, Policies, `resolveView`) is fundamentally substrate, or a separable layer above the truly-portable data format (deltas, content-addressing, signatures, negation), is a real long-horizon architectural question — one could imagine the format being the substrate and the query layer living above it. But the HyperSchema Schema already lives in rhizomatic, so for consistency the Schema Schema joining it is the smaller, parity-preserving move; the separability question can be its own discussion.

Referenced by Loam §21 (schema identity); related to #10 (the `loadSchema`/`publishSchemaClaims` naming lag).

tickets.title: Vocabulary: `loadSchema` / `publishSchemaClaims` operate on HyperSchemas, not Schemas
tickets.body:
Since the 0.3.0 L5 realignment, the at-rest schema-definition vocabulary is `rhizomatic.hyperschema.*`, and the conceptual split is **HyperSchema** (the gather program) vs **Schema** (the resolution program: `{ props: Map<field, Policy>, default: Policy }`). But the public API `loadSchema` and `publishSchemaClaims` load and publish **HyperSchemas** — the names predate the realignment and now lag their meaning.

This surfaced while designing Loam's §21 (schema identity & versioning), which makes a Loam-level Schema a first-class entity distinct from the HyperSchema. Loam is renaming its own `schema:` entity prefix to remove the blur on its side, but the substrate API names remain a source of confusion: a reader of `loadSchema` reasonably expects it to load a Schema (a resolution program), when it in fact loads a HyperSchema (a gather program).

rhizomatic is frozen/normative, so this is filed for discussion, not as a Loam workaround. Options:
- **(a)** rename to `loadHyperSchema` / `publishHyperSchemaClaims` — breaking API change (conformance vectors + version bump + migration guidance);
- **(b)** add the accurate names as aliases and deprecate the old ones;
- **(c)** document the lag and keep the names as stable API even though the vocabulary moved under them.

No urgency — Loam routes around it in prose today (§21 records the seam). Raising per Myk's request to resolve the last naming detail.

_Filed from the Loam §21 design review._

edges.contract: the settled loadSchema/publishSchemaClaims API naming that #11 designs its Schema publish/load parity against

tickets.title: Composable set-difference (and intersection) term operators over delta-sets
tickets.body:
## Summary

Complete the first-class **set algebra over delta-sets**: `Term` has `union` but no `difference` (minus) or `intersect`. Add composable `difference` and `intersect` term operators over `dset`-sort terms, symmetric with `union`.

## Motivation (from Loam's container work, SPEC §24/§27)

Loam is building **containers**: a container's *membership* is a delta-query — a `Term` that evaluates to a `dset` (exactly the shape `offeredLens` already uses). Organizing a store into containers, some **excluded** (the "sandbox" property), needs the read scope to be *"the union of the active containers **minus** the excluded ones"* — i.e. **set algebra over delta-sets**: `∪`, `∩`, `∖`.

Today the `Term` algebra (`src/eval.ts` `Term` union) has **`union` as a first-class op, but not difference or intersection**. That asymmetry is the gap: you can compose containers by union but not by exclusion or overlap, which are exactly what "some containers are sandboxed/excluded" and "what's shared between two containers" require.

## Current state (what I verified)

The one way to express exclusion today is the reflective `inView` predicate + `not`:

```
select{
  pred: not( inView{ term: T, field: "id", extract: { kind: "field", field: "id" } } ),
  of: input
}
```

= `input ∖ T` (by delta id). This works for the simple single-level case, but three constraints stop it from being the general set-difference the container model needs:

1. **Depth-1 stratification** (`inView.term` may not itself contain `inView` — enforced at parse). So you **cannot difference against a term that is itself a difference** — i.e. containers defined *relative to other containers* (A = base ∖ B, where B = base ∖ C) don't compose. This is the blocker for composable/nested containers.
2. **`dset`-sort only** for the referenced term.
3. **Keyed only on `id`/`author`** (the two `inView.field` options), not a general set operation over the whole delta.

`union` has none of these limits — it's a plain, nestable, whole-delta term op. Difference/intersection should match it.

## Requested

Two new `Term` ops, symmetric with `union`, over `dset`-sort operands, composable/nestable to any depth (whole-delta, deduped by content-addressed id):

```ts
| { readonly kind: "difference"; readonly of: Term; readonly without: Term }   // of.set ∖ without.set
| { readonly kind: "intersect";  readonly left: Term; readonly right: Term }   // left.set ∩ right.set
```

`difference` is the concretely-blocking one (exclusion / the sandbox property). `intersect` completes the boolean set algebra (you have `∪`; `∖` and `∩` finish it) and covers overlapping containers ("what's in both A and B").

## Scope / non-goals

Deliberately narrow. This is **not** asking for regex/substring string matching, bytes-target (mime/payload) predicates, a delta-intrinsic context/role, or `between` on the timestamp field — the existing `match` (author/timestamp/id with the `Cmp` vocabulary), `hasPointer`, and `and`/`or`/`not` already cover container-membership selection (authored-by, time-range, explicit-id-set, touching-entity, in-context, boolean combos all verified expressible). The single gap is composable **set difference** (and its natural companion, intersection).

## Context

Design-stage request for discussion, tied to Loam's §24 quarantine / §27 containers work (membership = a delta-query; sandbox = a container property; the scope is set algebra over containers). Happy to iterate on the shape — the essential need is *composable, nestable set-difference over dsets*, first-class like `union`.

tickets.title: Pin the Ed25519 verification criterion — the two witnesses accept different edge-case signatures
tickets.body:
## Summary

The two witnesses verify Ed25519 signatures under **different acceptance criteria**, and the spec
pins neither. For honestly-generated signatures the criteria agree, so every existing vector passes
in both — but on the documented edge cases (non-canonical point encodings, small-order components)
they are known to disagree. A delta exists that one conformant witness admits and the other refuses.
By this repo's own working agreement, that is the finding: *"when they disagree, the spec or the
vectors are underspecified."*

## What each witness does today

- **TypeScript** — `implementations/ts/src/sign.ts:42` calls `@noble/curves`' `ed25519.verify(sig,
  msg, pub)` with no options. noble's documented default is **ZIP215** verification: cofactored, and
  deliberately permissive about non-canonical encodings of R and A.
- **Rust** — `implementations/rust/src/sign.rs:88` calls `ed25519-dalek`'s **`verify_strict`**:
  cofactorless, rejecting non-canonical encodings and small-order components.

RFC 8032 underspecifies exactly this edge behavior, and the divergence between real libraries is
well documented — *"Taming the many EdDSAs"* and its companion test suite
[ed25519-speccheck](https://github.com/novifinancial/ed25519-speccheck) exist precisely to expose
it. ZIP215 and dalek-strict land on different sides of several speccheck cases.

(Signing is unaffected: RFC 8032 signing is deterministic and identical everywhere, which is why
`deltas-signed.json` reproduces byte-for-byte in both witnesses. The divergence is only in which
*inputs* verify.)

## Why this matters here

Verification is admission. `verifyDelta` gates what enters a store, and a federation of conformant
implementations that admit different delta sets does not converge — the one property the whole
substrate exists to guarantee. The failure needs no adversary to be a spec defect, but it has one
anyway: crafting an edge-case signature that splits two witnesses is exactly what speccheck's
vectors are.

The gap is invisible to the current suite by construction: `deltas-invalid.json` covers claims-shape
validation only, and `deltas-signed.json` covers honest signatures, which every criterion accepts.

## The decision (Myk)

Pin **one** criterion in SPEC-1 (or its ERRATA). The two candidates:

- **(a) ZIP215** — designed for exactly this requirement: an unambiguous, batch-compatible criterion
  that all verifiers can agree on, chosen by consensus systems (Zcash, Zebra) because admission
  divergence is a chain split for them, as it is a federation split for us. TS already complies;
  Rust would move to a ZIP215 implementation (e.g. the `ed25519-consensus` crate) or explicit
  checks.
- **(b) Strict** — reject non-canonical encodings and small-order components at the boundary. This
  matches the house aesthetic (NFC is validated, never repaired; ids must recompute; the boundary
  refuses weirdness rather than normalizing it). Rust already complies; TS is a one-line change
  (`zip215: false`). The cost: "strict" must then be specified precisely in our own words, since
  strictness varies subtly between libraries — the spec text, not any library's default, becomes the
  criterion.

Either pin is sound. The unpinned state is the only wrong option.

## Fix shape

1. SPEC-1 (or ERRATA) states the criterion normatively, in full — not by reference to a library.
2. Speccheck-derived vectors join `l0-delta/` (a `deltas-sig-edge.json`: signature bytes + expected
   verdict), so the criterion is machine-checked in every witness forever.
3. The non-complying witness changes its call. One line either way.

## Relation to #19

The Elixir witness (#19) is written from spec + vectors alone, so its sign/verify slice cannot be
built until this pin exists — there is no behavior to conform to, and Erlang's `:crypto` (OpenSSL)
would otherwise land on a third acceptance set distinct from both current witnesses. This issue
gates that slice of #19 and nothing else in it.

_Filed from the Elixir-witness design review (#19)._

🤖 Drafted with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) at Myk's request.

edges.contract: the normatively pinned Ed25519 acceptance criterion plus the speccheck-derived deltas-sig-edge vectors that the Elixir witness sign/verify slice conforms to

tickets.title: An Elixir witness — the first citizen written from spec + vectors alone (L0 first)
tickets.body:
## Summary

Add `implementations/elixir` — a **third witness**, starting (and possibly stopping) at **Level 0 —
Format**. Written from `spec/` and `vectors/` **alone**, without reading the existing witnesses.

## Motivation: the central claim has never actually been tested

SPEC-0 §5 makes a strong claim, and names this language while making it:

> Rhizomatic ships a **conformance suite, not a reference implementation**... An implementation is
> conformant if it passes the vectors. The TypeScript implementation is one conformant citizen; **it
> has no special authority. Implementations in Elixir**, Rust, the browser, or an embedded sensor are
> equally first-class — this is what "trivial to implement in unexpected places" means operationally.

Both existing witnesses grew up *in this repo*, in lockstep, by the same hand, with the spec, the
vectors, and the code co-evolving slice by slice. Whenever TS and Rust disagreed, the spec got fixed
and both got fixed with it. That is exactly the discipline CLAUDE.md prescribes, and it worked — but
it means neither witness has ever been a *test* of the conformance claim. They are its **authors**.
Ambiguity that both happened to resolve the same way, because the same mind resolved it twice, is
invisible from inside.

A witness written **from the spec and the vectors only** — by an implementer who cannot open
`implementations/ts` when stuck — is the first real experiment. The question it answers is not "does
Elixir work?" It is: **is the conformance suite sufficient to conform to?**

Every place the answer is no is a finding, exactly in the sense the working agreement already means
it: *"When they disagree, the spec or the vectors are underspecified, and that is a finding, not a
nuisance."*

## Why Elixir, and why now

**The BEAM disagrees differently.** The `float_roundtrip` defect is the model case: `serde_json`'s
fast float path parsed decimals up to 1 ULP off, fracturing canonical-bytes parity with JS, caught by
`float-f16-min-subnormal`. It was found because Rust's host disagreed with JS's host. But TS and Rust
are closer relatives than that story suggests — both are IEEE-754-double natives with the same
ergonomic grain. The BEAM's grain is foreign in ways that land exactly on the canonical-bytes path:
integers and floats are distinct terms (`1` and `1.0` are different map keys, and JSON-profile
parsing yields integers that must become floats under the floats-only rule); there are no native
f16/f32 types, so the RFC 8949 shortest-float ladder — down to f16 subnormals at 2^-24 — is
hand-rolled with nothing to lean on; and strings are binaries with their own normalization plumbing.
**A third host that disagrees in unfamiliar ways is worth far more than a third implementation that
agrees for free.** It is the most likely place the next `float_roundtrip` is hiding.

**There is a real consumer waiting.** Kyber-BEAM (an Elixir/OTP agent harness) is being rewritten
onto Loam. It already keeps an append-only delta log as its source of truth and replays it to
reconstruct state — it converged on this architecture independently, which is its own small piece of
evidence. Its agent identity is a keypair, so it must author and **sign deltas in-process**: a
harness whose pitch is sovereignty cannot phone a Node sidecar to sign. That is precisely and only a
Level 0 need. The witness would be exercised by real use, not by vectors alone.

## The methodological constraint — this is the point, not a flourish

Write it from `spec/` + `vectors/` **only**. Do not read `implementations/ts` or
`implementations/rust` while implementing. When stuck, the resolution is to **fix `spec/` or
`vectors/`** — never to peek. Peeking silently converts the experiment into a port and destroys the
finding, which is the only thing here that can't be bought some other way.

(Reading the other witnesses *after* a slice is green, to compare notes, is fine and probably useful.)

The rule has a boundary, named here so the experiment's claims stay honest: the model that helps
write this witness has likely also read — or helped write — the existing ones, so shared priors can
resolve an ambiguity identically a third time without any peeking. Mitigations, cheap and worth
taking: build each slice in a fresh context holding only `spec/` and `vectors/`; prefer a different
model family than wrote the corresponding TS/Rust code for the first pass at each slice; and count
only vector failures as evidence that the suite is sufficient — a clean green run is a weaker result
than it looks.

**Corollary: a Rustler NIF binding the Rust witness is an explicit non-goal.** It would be the
cheapest possible path to green — and it would yield three *bindings* and two *witnesses*. The
independence is the entire value; buying it out is buying nothing.

## Scope: Level 0, and the ladder means that's allowed

§5.1 blesses stopping: *"Level 0 — Format: Can parse, canonically serialize, and content-address
deltas (SPEC-1)."* That is complete, first-class citizenship — not a partial implementation.

L0 is: canonical CBOR (shortest-float per RFC 8949 §4.2.1 down to f16 subnormals at 2^-24, floats
only — no integer major types, `-0.0` → `+0.0`, definite lengths, bytewise map-key ordering), blake3
content addressing, Ed25519 sign/verify, NFC **validated at the boundary and never repaired**, b64u,
and delta-set ops.

The L0 gate, precisely: `l0-delta/` (`cbor-primitives`, `deltas`, `deltas-signed`, `deltas-bytes`,
`deltas-invalid`) and `l0-pack/`, with `keys/` supplying the deterministic seeds. `set-digest` is
provisional (ERRATA D10) and gates as such. One prerequisite lands first: the exact
signature-verification acceptance criterion is unpinned at the edges — the two existing witnesses sit
on different criteria — and is being settled in #20; the sign/verify slice of this witness waits on
that pin and nothing else does.

Climb to L1+ only when a consumer needs it — Kyber signs in-process at L0 and consumes resolution
from a Loam gateway over the wire; if latency ever demands local materializations, L2 is a dial on
the same ladder, not a fork.

## What this exposes in the repo itself (possibly the better half of the deliverable)

1. **`tools/check-all.mjs` hardcodes two witnesses** and signs off with *"Both witnesses green. The
   parity contract holds."* A third makes the parity runner's shape dishonest. It should be
   N-witness, and parity should be a relation over a set, not a pair.
2. **There is no documented bring-up path for a new witness.** The vectors are loadable JSON with
   hex-encoded bytes — genuinely portable, which is most of the battle — but nothing says *which*
   vectors constitute the L0 set, what order to attack them in, or what "done at L0" means. If an
   outsider really is a first-class citizen, that path should exist for them. This ticket produces it
   as a byproduct, and the byproduct may outlive the Elixir code.
3. **No witness declares its conformance level anywhere machine-readable.** With two at L4 it didn't
   matter. With a third at L0 it starts to.

## Open questions

1. **In-repo or out?** `implementations/elixir` alongside its siblings keeps the lockstep discipline;
   but an in-repo witness is only half an outsider, and outsider-ness is what's being tested.
   *Recommendation: in-repo, with the no-peeking rule standing in for genuine distance.*
2. **Lockstep across uneven levels.** CLAUDE.md says *"never let one race more than a slice ahead"* —
   written when both witnesses were climbing together. An L0-only witness is permanently behind at
   L1+ by design, and the ladder says that's fine. *Suggested amendment: lockstep binds per level,
   not per repo.* Small, but it should be said out loud rather than discovered.
3. **blake3, and an honest confrontation with the irony.** Elixir's `:crypto` covers Ed25519 via
   OpenSSL, but blake3 on the BEAM most likely means a Rust NIF. Is that still an independent
   witness? *I'd argue yes* — blake3 is a shared external standard with its own published test
   vectors, not rhizomatic's contribution, and the independence that matters lives in
   canonicalization, validation, and the algebra. But it's worth stating up front rather than
   tripping over later, and it's worth being suspicious of the moment the same reasoning gets reached
   for a second time.
4. **Packaging.** Published to Hex, or unpublished like the Rust witness's deliberate
   `publish = false`? (Kyber consumes L0 in-process for signing and a Loam gateway over the wire for
   everything else, so a git dep suffices for the foreseeable future.)
5. **The Unicode version behind NFC validation.** "Is this string NFC?" is answered by a Unicode data
   table, and the three witnesses would consult three: the host ICU (TS), the `unicode-normalization`
   crate's pinned tables (Rust), OTP's own (Elixir). Code points unassigned in one version pass
   through NFC unchanged — and validate — until a later version assigns them a decomposition; a
   string built on that gap is admitted by one witness and refused by another. The spec should pin a
   Unicode version or state the policy for unassigned code points; one sentence in SPEC-1 closes it.
6. **The host-boundary numeric policy.** SPEC-1 pins the bytes — numbers are floats only, no integer
   major types — but not what a witness does when handed a *native integer term* at claim
   construction, a question only askable in hosts that have one (Erlang, Python, Ruby; JS cannot ask
   it, which is why the spec is silent). Three candidate policies: reject, always-coerce, or
   coerce-iff-exactly-representable. The spec's own NFC rationale decides it — *"in-memory equality
   is thereby byte equality everywhere"* fails on the BEAM if integer terms enter claims (`42 =:=
   42.0` is false; they are distinct map keys), and always-coerce silently destroys integers above
   2^53. So: **reject integer terms at construction**, with one blessed coercion point — the
   JSON-profile parser, where an integer token is unambiguously a float spelling (JSON has one
   number type, and the vectors' own `"timestamp": 0` already requires it). Values not exactly
   representable as f64 are rejected everywhere, always. The vectors can pin the profile half — a
   vector asserting the `42` and `42.0` spellings produce identical canonical hex belongs in
   `l0-delta/` — but the native-term half is structurally invisible to JSON vector files, so it
   lives as SPEC-1 prose plus per-witness boundary tests, carried by the bring-up-path document
   (deliverable 2 above). Sentence in SPEC-1, worth writing now. (If a consumer someday needs true
   arithmetic integers or decimals, the path is not repinning the number model — it is an additive,
   shape-distinguishable target kind, the exact precedent the `bytes` target set in 0.4: no
   migration, old deltas untouched.)

## Non-goals

Levels 1–4 (a later ticket, if a consumer ever needs them). A Rustler binding of the Rust witness.
Anything Phoenix, LiveView, or OTP-flavored — that is the consumer's business, and the witness should
be boring, pure, and dependency-light, per *"Boring at L0–L2... the kind of code strangers rewrite in
five languages."* Publishing to Hex before it's green.

## Context

Filed from a Loam design discussion about a possible Elixir Loam. The conclusion there was that
**Loam**-in-Elixir is premature — Loam has no vectors and no conformance ladder, so "parity" would
have nothing to measure against and would quietly make TypeScript the reference implementation, which
is the one thing SPEC-0 §5 forbids. But **rhizomatic**-in-Elixir at L0 is the opposite: well
specified, already vectored, explicitly invited by the spec, and with a real consumer waiting.

Nothing in Loam blocks on this.

_Filed from the Loam / Kyber Elixir design discussion._

🤖 Drafted with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) at Myk's request.
